ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general orange juice drink entry; the pulp-containing variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GG covers fruit nectars and fruit drinks broadly; no dedicated code exists for orange juice drink with pulp. Closest defensible category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for orange juice drink with pulp; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one glass of approximately 250 ml, providing around 103 kcal and 23.6 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Orange juice drink with pulp — HPB Singapore Foodo Database (D03070026)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D03070026 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to 0.0 as the total fat is 0.0 g/100g (confirmed zero by formulation — this is a fat-free beverage). Potassium estimated at 180 mg/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for orange juice drink (FDC ID 1101723) as a secondary cross-reference. Vitamin C estimated at 40 mg/100g based on USDA FoodData Central orange juice drink data as a secondary cross-reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 50 for orange juice drink is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for orange juice (reconstituted from concentrate), which reports GI values in the range 46–57 (mean ~50). The pulp-containing variant is treated as equivalent for GI purposes as the pulp fibre content (0.32 g/100g) is insufficient to materially alter the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 25.73) / 100 = 12.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.44 g/100g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage exceeds the 5 g/100g SSB threshold; independently associated with elevated blood pressure risk regardless of low sodium (7 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to its high free sugar content (9.44 g/100g); the AHA 2018 guidelines link sugar-sweetened beverage consumption to elevated blood pressure independently of sodium intake, and the negligible fibre (0.32 g/100g) provides no meaningful mitigation — whole oranges are the preferred alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.44 g sugar/100g and only 0.32 g fibre/100g; liquid form accelerates glucose absorption and produces rapid postprandial glycaemic excursions
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: the high free sugar (9.44 g/100g) in liquid form is absorbed rapidly, the GI of 50 combined with a glycemic load of 12.9 per 250 ml serving represents a significant glucose challenge, and the minimal pulp fibre does not meaningfully slow absorption — whole oranges with intact fibre matrix are strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (9.44 g/100g) in a liquid beverage is associated with elevated triglycerides, hepatic lipogenesis, and increased CVD risk; fat content is zero and sodium is low (7 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this beverage: while fat (0.0 g/100g) and sodium (7 mg/100g) are not concerns, the high free sugar (9.44 g/100g) in liquid form promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and hepatic fat accumulation per AHA dietary guidelines, and regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased CVD mortality risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (9.44 g/100g) in liquid form promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; negligible fibre (0.32 g/100g) provides no anti-inflammatory offset
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as the high free sugar load in liquid form promotes systemic inflammation via advanced glycation end-products and pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; the vitamin C content (~40 mg/100g) does not offset the pro-inflammatory sugar burden, and whole oranges are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Orange Juice Drink with Pulp is a processed beverage made from reconstituted orange juice concentrate, water, added sweeteners, and suspended orange pulp pieces. It is not pure squeezed orange juice but a formulated juice drink that typically contains less than 100% juice content. The beverage has a sweet, tangy citrus flavour with a slightly textured mouthfeel from the pulp particles. It is served chilled and is commonly consumed as a breakfast drink, snack beverage, or refreshment. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 41 kcal, 0.63 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 10.29 g carbohydrate of which 9.44 g is sugar, 0.32 g dietary fibre, and 7 mg sodium. The fat content is confirmed zero by product formulation — this is a fat-free beverage. The high free sugar content (9.44 g/100g) classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under both HPB Singapore and WHO definitions. Despite the presence of pulp, the fibre content (0.32 g/100g) is negligible and does not meaningfully slow glucose absorption. The glycemic index is approximately 50 (low category) but the glycemic load per 250 ml serving is 12.9, representing a significant glucose challenge. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, Hypertension, or Rheumatoid Arthritis due to its high free sugar load in liquid form. Vitamin C content is approximately 40 mg/100g. Whole oranges are consistently preferred over this product for health-conscious consumers as they provide fibre, slower glucose absorption, and equivalent vitamin C without the concentrated sugar load.

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03070026, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). GI value estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for reconstituted orange juice concentrate drink. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are all confirmed zero by product formulation given total fat is 0.0 g/100g. Potassium and vitamin C are secondary estimates from USDA FoodData Central orange juice drink data and are clearly documented as estimates.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — this is a fat-free beverage with 0.0 g total fat per HPB source; saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero as a consequence. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record (confirmed zero per HPB). cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived beverage with no animal-derived fat. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB juice drink entry are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg (180 mg/100g) and vitamin_c_mg (40 mg/100g) are secondary estimates cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central orange juice drink data (FDC ID 1101723) and are clearly labelled as estimates in nutrient_source notes. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for reconstituted orange juice concentrate; the pulp variant is treated as equivalent given negligible fibre content. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (50 × 10.29 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.5 serving factor) / 100 = 12.9 for a 250 ml serving. This beverage qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under HPB Singapore and WHO definitions (free sugar > 5 g/100g in a beverage). Multilingual local names are not applicable for this generic international beverage product; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine-specific naming requirement applies.
